content.text: In classical MLOps, iteration usually means retraining: you collect labelled data, retrain the model, run offline evals, and redeploy. In LLMOps, iteration is

. Because the model is a third-party foundation model that you do not own, you have four distinct levers — prompt engineering, retrieval tuning, fine-tuning, and model swap — each with a different cost, risk profile, and expected quality gain. Choosing the right lever for each failure mode is the core skill of the Iterate phase.

The Monitoring phase produces signals; the Iteration phase interprets them and decides what to change. Not every signal warrants the same response. This table maps each monitoring signal to its most likely root cause and the recommended first-line iteration action.
Judge score drops without prompt change
Pin model version; re-run offline eval suite
Judge score drops after prompt change
Revert prompt; add failing case to eval corpus
Run offline eval on new distribution; update few-shot examples
Adversarial campaign or policy scope creep
Audit blocked queries; tighten or relax guardrail rules
Audit prompt construction; trim retrieval chunks
Add length constraint to system prompt; use output formatting
Poor answers for specific topic class
Fine-tune on that topic class; add domain-specific RAG corpus
The cheapest and fastest lever. A prompt change takes minutes and can be rolled back instantly. It is the
thing to try for almost every failure mode. The key discipline is treating prompts as code: version-controlled, tested against an eval set before deployment, and reviewed like a pull request.

If the model's base knowledge is sufficient but the answers are factually wrong on your domain, the problem is almost always retrieval: wrong chunks, stale corpus, or a mismatch between the query embedding model and the document embedding model. RAG iteration involves tuning chunking strategy, re-ranking, or expanding the corpus.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class RetrievalConfig:
    chunk_size_tokens: int   = 512
    chunk_overlap_tokens: int = 64
    top_k: int               = 5
    reranker: str            = "none"  # "none" | "cross-encoder" | "llm"
    embedding_model: str     = "text-embedding-3-small"

@dataclass
class RetrievalEvalResult:
    config_label: str
    recall_at_k: float        # fraction of gold docs retrieved in top-k
    mrr: float                # mean reciprocal rank
    context_token_p90: int    # p90 prompt-token contribution from retrieved chunks

def compare_retrieval_configs(
    queries: List[str],
    gold_doc_ids: List[List[str]],
    configs: List[RetrievalConfig],
    retrieve_fn,   # (query, config) -> List[str] (doc_ids)
) -> List[RetrievalEvalResult]:
    results = []
    for cfg in configs:
        recalls, rrs = [], []
        for query, gold in zip(queries, gold_doc_ids):
            retrieved = retrieve_fn(query, cfg)
            gold_set = set(gold)
            hit = [1 if d in gold_set else 0 for d in retrieved]
            recalls.append(sum(hit) / max(len(gold), 1))
            rr = next((1/(i+1) for i, h in enumerate(hit) if h), 0)
            rrs.append(rr)
        results.append(RetrievalEvalResult(
            config_label=f"k={cfg.top_k} chunk={cfg.chunk_size_tokens}",
            recall_at_k=sum(recalls)/len(recalls),
            mrr=sum(rrs)/len(rrs),
            context_token_p90=0,   # fill from real profiling
        ))
    return sorted(results, key=lambda r: r.mrr, reverse=True)

Fine-tuning is warranted when prompt and RAG changes have plateaued and a consistent pattern of failure exists across at least 100–200 examples. It carries the highest cost (GPU hours, data preparation, deployment pipeline) and the longest cycle time (days to weeks). Use it when you need the model to adopt a consistent style, format, or domain vocabulary that cannot be expressed efficiently in a prompt.
Insufficient signal; collect more data first
Failure fixed by adding 2–3 few-shot examples
Style/format must be highly consistent across all outputs
Fine-tuned model can omit few-shot → shorter prompts
Switch to a tunable model first

Switching to a different foundation model is the most disruptive iteration. All prompts must be re-tested; latency, cost, and quality profiles shift simultaneously. Treat a model swap as a major release: run the full offline eval suite on the candidate model before touching production, gate the canary at 1 % traffic for at least 24 hours, and have a rollback plan that restores the previous model in under five minutes.

attrs.alt: LLMOps quality flywheel: Deploy → Monitor → Analyse → Iterate → Evaluate → Deploy, cycling continuously.

content.text: Each turn of the flywheel above produces a faster, cheaper next turn. Early iterations fix gross failures with blunt tools (prompt rewrites, corpus additions). Later iterations address subtle quality gaps with targeted fine-tuning or model swaps. The flywheel is healthy when each cycle produces a measurable quality improvement with fewer side effects — and it breaks when any stage is skipped. Deploying without evaluation, or monitoring without acting, stalls the cycle.
Comparing Iterations Safely: Shadow and A/B Testing
No iteration should be promoted to full production traffic without first being compared against the incumbent. Two patterns are commonly used.
The new system processes every production request in parallel but its response is
to the user. Shadow responses are evaluated offline. This is low-risk but consumes 2× the compute.

import asyncio
from typing import Optional

async def shadow_request(
    prompt: str,
    production_fn,    # current system
    shadow_fn,        # candidate system
    emit_trace_fn,    # stores (prompt, prod_reply, shadow_reply) for eval
) -> str:
    """Returns the production reply; shadow runs concurrently but is unused.""\"
    prod_task   = asyncio.create_task(production_fn(prompt))
    shadow_task = asyncio.create_task(shadow_fn(prompt))

    prod_reply, shadow_reply = await asyncio.gather(prod_task, shadow_task)
    asyncio.create_task(emit_trace_fn(prompt, prod_reply, shadow_reply))
    return prod_reply          # user sees only production

A fraction of traffic (1–10 %) is routed to the new system; users in the canary group see the new response. Quality is compared using online eval scores and explicit user feedback. Canary testing gives
user signal but exposes a subset of users to potential regressions.

import random
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Awaitable

@dataclass
class ABRouter:
    canary_fraction: float        # e.g. 0.05 for 5 %
    production_fn: Callable
    canary_fn: Callable
    record_assignment_fn: Callable  # (trace_id, group) -> None

    async def route(self, prompt: str, trace_id: str) -> str:
        if random.random() < self.canary_fraction:
            group = "canary"
            reply = await self.canary_fn(prompt)
        else:
            group = "production"
            reply = await self.production_fn(prompt)
        self.record_assignment_fn(trace_id, group)
        return reply

    def should_promote(
        self,
        canary_score: float,
        prod_score: float,
        min_sample_size: int,
        canary_n: int,
    ) -> bool:
        """Promote if canary beats production and sample is sufficient.""\"
        return canary_n >= min_sample_size and canary_score > prod_score + 0.1
As the product matures, ad-hoc prompt edits become a liability. Iteration governance formalises who can change what, under what conditions, and with what evidence.
(model swap, fine-tuning deployment). Each tier has different approval and eval requirements.
: every change must pass a defined eval threshold before staging. Store the eval run ID and result in the prompt registry alongside the version metadata.
: define a maximum time-to-rollback for each risk tier. High-risk changes must be rollback-able in under 5 minutes (hot swap via feature flag); low-risk changes within one business day.
: maintain a human-readable changelog that links each production version to its eval results, rollout date, canary stats, and the monitoring signal that triggered it.
The most dangerous iteration is the undocumented one. A prompt change without an eval run and a change log entry is indistinguishable from a regression when debugging a quality incident at 2 AM.
Fast iteration cycles surface quality improvements quickly but also surface regressions quickly. The right cadence depends on the maturity of the system and the cost of a bad user experience.
Offline eval on 200 cases + 24 h canary
Full eval suite + 72 h canary + shadow
An iteration is only useful if it demonstrably improves quality without introducing regressions. The right measurement framework tracks three quantities across every iteration cycle.
A delta metric compares the candidate system against the incumbent on the same eval set. All three delta metrics must be positive (or within an acceptable tolerance) before promotion.
Every iteration cycle should run a

— a fixed set of eval cases that exercise previously fixed failure modes. A case is added to the regression suite the moment a bug is found and fixed. The suite grows with every iteration cycle and makes it impossible to re-introduce a known defect undetected.
: 20 cases per known failure class; at least 100 cases total before any fine-tuning deployment.
: each case stores the original user query, the expected answer or expected quality indicator, and the monitoring signal that surfaced it.
: CI/CD pipeline runs the regression suite on every PR that touches a prompt file or model config; PR is blocked if any case drops below a defined threshold.

When a quality regression is detected in production, the first debugging step is identifying what changed. Prompt diffing — comparing the current production prompt against the previous version line-by-line — surfaces the change that most likely caused the regression.
Root-cause analysis combines prompt diffing with
: compute the judge score separately for each topic cluster in the eval set. If the score drops only in the "refunds" cluster after a prompt change, the change is the root cause.
Several common iteration mistakes slow the flywheel or introduce hidden regressions.

: appending instructions to a prompt every time a new failure surfaces without ever auditing the whole prompt. Prompts grow past 2 000 tokens, become contradictory, and hurt latency. Schedule a prompt audit every quarter.

: using the same examples for both prompt engineering ("let me add this example to the prompt") and evaluation. The eval set measures generalization; once an example is in the prompt it can no longer do that job. Keep the eval set locked and separate.
: a wording change in the system prompt has caused P1 incidents. Every change that reaches production must go through at least a 1 % canary, no exceptions.

: fine-tuning on fewer than 50 labelled examples produces models that overfit to the training distribution and fail on slight variations. Accumulate at least 200 diverse, high-quality examples before starting a fine-tuning run.
(versioned ID) means a provider update can silently change behaviour overnight. Always pin the versioned ID in production.

Iteration in LLMOps is a four-lever system: prompt engineering (fast, cheap, first resort), retrieval tuning (data-quality lever), fine-tuning (expensive but permanent), and model swap (high-disruption, highest ceiling). Each lever is triggered by specific monitoring signals, tested in shadow or canary mode, and gated by offline eval scores before promotion. The iteration flywheel — Deploy → Monitor → Analyse → Iterate → Evaluate → Deploy — is the heartbeat of a production LLM system: when it turns smoothly, quality compounds; when any stage is skipped, regressions accumulate silently until they become incidents.
